subject: ap-calculus-bc
theme: Limits are the foundation of calculus — they let us rigorously define instantaneous change and continuity by asking what value a function approaches, not what it equals.

core_concepts.title: The Definition of a Limit
core_concepts.detail: A limit $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$ means $f(x)$ gets arbitrarily close to $L$ as $x$ approaches $c$, without $x$ ever equaling $c$ — the function value at $c$ is irrelevant to the limit.

core_concepts.title: One-Sided Limits & Existence
core_concepts.detail: A two-sided limit exists only when the left-hand limit $\lim_{x \to c^-} f(x)$ and right-hand limit $\lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)$ are equal. If they differ, the two-sided limit does not exist.

core_concepts.title: Types of Discontinuity
core_concepts.detail: Removable: limit exists but $f(c) \neq \lim_{x \to c} f(x)$. Jump: left and right limits exist but differ. Infinite: function grows without bound near $c$ (vertical asymptote).

core_concepts.title: Continuity at a Point
core_concepts.detail: A function is continuous at $x = c$ when all three conditions hold: $f(c)$ exists, $\lim_{x \to c} f(x)$ exists, and $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)$.

core_concepts.title: The Squeeze Theorem
core_concepts.detail: If $g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x)$ near $c$ and $\lim_{x \to c} g(x) = \lim_{x \to c} h(x) = L$, then $\lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L$. Used to evaluate limits of functions bounded by simpler ones.

core_concepts.title: Intermediate Value Theorem
core_concepts.detail: If $f$ is continuous on $[a, b]$ and $d$ is between $f(a)$ and $f(b)$, then there exists at least one $c \in (a, b)$ with $f(c) = d$. Used to prove equations have solutions.

core_concepts.title: Limits at Infinity
core_concepts.detail: Limits at infinity describe end behavior: $\lim_{x \to \infty} f(x) = L$ means the graph approaches horizontal asymptote $y = L$. For rational functions, compare degrees of numerator and denominator.

core_concepts.title: Key Trigonometric Limits
core_concepts.detail: Two must-memorize limits: $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{\sin x}{x} = 1$ and $\lim_{x \to 0} \frac{1 - \cos x}{x} = 0$. These are foundational for deriving trig derivatives.

formulas.name: Limit Notation
formulas.description: Reads 'the limit of f(x) as x approaches c is L.' It means f(x) gets as close as you want to L when x is close (but not equal) to c.
formulas.example: For $f(x) = x^2$, $\lim_{x \to 3} x^2 = 9$. The function approaches 9 as $x$ approaches 3.

formulas.name: One-Sided Limit Existence Condition
formulas.katex_string: \lim_{x \to c} f(x) \text{ exists} \iff \lim_{x \to c^-} f(x) = \lim_{x \to c^+} f(x)
formulas.description: A two-sided limit exists if and only if the function approaches the same value from both left and right.
formulas.example: For a step function that equals 1 for $x < 0$ and 2 for $x > 0$: left limit at 0 is 1, right limit is 2, so $\lim_{x \to 0}$ DNE.

formulas.name: Continuity at a Point
formulas.katex_string: f(c) \text{ exists},\quad \lim_{x \to c} f(x) \text{ exists},\quad \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = f(c)
formulas.description: All three must be satisfied for f to be continuous at x = c. The function must be defined there, the limit must exist, and they must match.
formulas.example: If $f(x) = \frac{x^2 - 1}{x - 1}$ and $f(1) = 2$, then $f$ is continuous at $x = 1$ since the limit equals 2 and $f(1) = 2$.

formulas.name: Squeeze Theorem
formulas.katex_string: g(x) \leq f(x) \leq h(x) \text{ and } \lim_{x \to c} g(x) = \lim_{x \to c} h(x) = L \implies \lim_{x \to c} f(x) = L
formulas.description: If f is sandwiched between two functions that both approach L, then f must also approach L.
formulas.example: To show $\lim_{x \to 0} x^2 \sin(1/x) = 0$: since $-x^2 \leq x^2 \sin(1/x) \leq x^2$ and both outer limits are 0, the limit is 0.

formulas.name: Intermediate Value Theorem
formulas.katex_string: f \text{ continuous on } [a,b],\; d \in [f(a), f(b)] \implies \exists\, c \in (a,b) \text{ s.t. } f(c) = d
formulas.description: A continuous function cannot skip values — it must hit every y-value between its endpoints. Used to prove equations have solutions.
formulas.example: If $f(0) = -1$ and $f(2) = 5$ and $f$ is continuous, then by IVT there exists $c \in (0,2)$ where $f(c) = 0$.

formulas.name: Key Trigonometric Limits
formulas.description: These two limits must be memorized. They appear directly in limit problems and are the basis for proving that the derivative of sin x is cos x.

exam_tip: On the BC exam, always verify that a limit is in the form $\frac{0}{0}$ or $\frac{\infty}{\infty}$ before attempting L'Hôpital's Rule, and when using IVT you must explicitly state that $f$ is continuous on the interval.
